title: Mediterranean Lemon Oregano Roast Chicken

ingredients.name: chicken, bone-in pieces (legs, thighs, or whole cut up)

subs.name: The same marinade works beautifully on a whole fish (pomfret or sea bass) roasted at 200°C for 20–25 minutes; reduce the marinade time to 30 minutes for fish
Any neutral oil works for the marinade function; the olive oil contributes flavour to the crust, so mild oils are preferred over strongly flavoured ones
Two tablespoons of white wine vinegar and a teaspoon of lemon extract give a similar sharpness when fresh lemons are unavailable
Dried rosemary (half the quantity) or a couple of sprigs of fresh thyme; the oregano is the more essential herb here and should not be reduced

ingredients.name: potato, cut into wedges (for roasting alongside)

steps.text: Mince or finely grate the garlic. Whisk together the olive oil, lemon juice, lemon zest, garlic, oregano, thyme, black pepper, and a generous amount of salt in a large bowl until well combined. Add the chicken pieces and turn them thoroughly to coat every surface. If time allows, leave to marinate in the refrigerator for 1–4 hours.

Preheat the oven to 200°C. Place the chicken pieces skin-side up in a single layer in a roasting tin or baking tray. If using potato wedges, tuck them around the chicken and season them with salt, a drizzle of olive oil, and a pinch of oregano. Pour any remaining marinade over everything.

Roast at 200°C for 45–50 minutes until the chicken skin is deeply golden and blistered, and the juices run completely clear when you pierce the thickest part with a knife. The potato wedges (if used) should be soft inside and golden-edged. Baste the chicken with the pan juices once at the halfway point for extra flavour.

Let the chicken rest in the tin for 5 minutes before serving — this allows the juices to settle back into the meat so the first cut doesn't lose them all. Spoon the golden roasting juices over the chicken and serve with the potatoes, a Greek salad, or warm flatbread.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → The same marinade works beautifully on a whole fish (pomfret or sea bass) roasted at 200°C for 20–25 minutes; reduce the marinade time to 30 minutes for fish; rosemary → Dried rosemary (half the quantity) or a couple of sprigs of fresh thyme; the oregano is the more essential herb here and should not be reduced; lemon → Two tablespoons of white wine vinegar and a teaspoon of lemon extract give a similar sharpness when fresh lemons are unavailable; olive-oil → Any neutral oil works for the marinade function; the olive oil contributes flavour to the crust, so mild oils are preferred over strongly flavoured ones
indian_kitchen_adaptation: An OTG (countertop oven) or a modern microwave-convection oven handles this recipe perfectly. For a stovetop version, marinate as directed, then sear the chicken in a kadai over high heat for 5 minutes per side, add 2 tablespoons of water, cover tightly, and cook over very low heat for 30–35 minutes until cooked through — it won't have the same crisp skin but the flavour will be excellent. Bone-in chicken pieces are sold at all Indian meat counters. Dried oregano is available in larger supermarkets and online.
